chunk_summary: Responsive feeding emphasizes caregiver-child interaction during meals, promoting hunger and satiety cues, structured routines, and gradual self-feeding development to prevent feeding delays in children aged 6–24 months.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of responsive feeding and its role in preventing feeding delays.
Learn strategies to establish structured mealtime routines and manage common feeding challenges.
Identify caregiver behaviors that support self-feeding and texture progression in children.
Recognize and address caregiver misconceptions about feeding practices.
key_facts: Responsive feeding involves recognizing hunger and satiety cues and responding promptly and appropriately.
Meals should be time-limited to 20–30 minutes and offered at planned intervals to avoid grazing.
Introduce safe finger foods around 9 months to encourage self-feeding and expect mess as part of learning.
Repeated exposure to new foods, paired with familiar staples, helps reduce food refusal.
When appetite is low, schedule milk feeds after solids and avoid screen-based feeding.
Caregiver stress and beliefs, such as fear of choking, can hinder feeding progress and should be addressed.
tags_mesh: Feeding and Eating Disorders of Childhood
reference_quote: WHO counselling guidance emphasizes feeding infants directly and assisting older children, encouraging eating but not forcing, talking to the child and maintaining eye contact, and minimizing distractions.
